dependencies.depends_on_spec: Bootstrap initialization (T-001, T-002) must be complete before user management and OAuth onboarding can be implemented, as they depend on the admin user and bootstrap token infrastructure.
User management (T-003) and OAuth onboarding (T-004) must be complete before API key management and CLI session storage can be implemented, as keys and sessions depend on user records.
API key management (T-006, T-007) and CLI token storage (T-005) must be complete before cross-cutting concerns (error handling, routing, multi-tenancy, RBAC, TLS) can be fully verified.
All feature implementation groups (1–4) must be complete before the full test suite (T-012) is executed.
The full test suite (T-012) must pass before the Phase 1 checkpoint (T-013) can be signed off.

details: Implement first-initialization detection: check whether an admin user and bootstrap token already exist in the database; skip initialization if they do.
On first run: create the admin user record with provenance fields (repo_id, created_by, created_at).
Generate the bootstrap token from a minimum of 32 cryptographically secure random bytes encoded as base64url.
Hash the plaintext bootstrap token with bcrypt or argon2 and persist only the hash.
Apply a configurable expiry to the bootstrap token (env/config variable; default: 90 days from initialization time).
Print the plaintext bootstrap token to stdout exactly once; never persist the plaintext.
Write an integration test covering TC-001 (first-run happy path), TC-002 (idempotent second run), TC-003 (custom expiry), ECT-001 (no overwrite on re-run), and ST-001 (smoke).
Implement POST /v1/admin/bootstrap-token/rotate endpoint.
Validate the caller's bearer token; enforce admin role (return 403 / permission_denied for non-admins).
Immediately invalidate the old bootstrap token hash in the database with no grace period.
Generate a new bootstrap token (32 random bytes, base64url encoded); hash and persist only the hash.
Return HTTP 200 with the new plaintext token in the response body exactly once.
Ensure the old token is rejected as token_invalid on any subsequent request (no grace period).

Write tests for TC-004 (happy path), TC-005 (expired caller token), TC-006 (invalid token), TC-007 (non-admin), ECT-002 (old token rejected immediately), ECT-015 (plaintext not re-retrievable), and ST-006 (smoke).
Implement POST /v1/users endpoint (admin only).
Validate request body: username and email are required; oauth_provider and provider_account_id must both be present or both absent — return 422 / validation_error otherwise.
Return 409 / conflict if the email already exists (unless the existing account only lacks this oauth_provider, in which case link the provider per auto-merge logic — ECT-003).
Persist user record with fields: username, email, oauth_provider, provider_account_id, created_at, created_by, repo_id.
Return HTTP 201 with the created user record.
Implement PATCH /v1/users/{user_id} endpoint (admin only): apply partial updates; return 404 / user_not_found if user_id does not exist; return 200 with updated record on success.
Enforce admin-only access on both endpoints; return 403 / permission_denied for non-admins.
Write tests for TC-008 through TC-017 and ECT-003.
Implement the OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant (RFC 8628) server-side flow: device authorization endpoint returning device_code, user_code, and verification_uri.
Support four OAuth providers: GitHub, GitLab, Google, Keycloak. Request minimum email + profile scopes (or provider equivalent).
On authorization grant: if email is new, auto-provision user account and return session token (HTTP 200).
On authorization grant: if email exists, resume session and perform auto-merge if a new oauth_provider is presented for the same email.
Reject OAuth flow if provider does not supply a verified email address (ECT-004).
Implement CLI device authorization grant flow: call device authorization endpoint, print verification_uri and user_code to terminal, poll token endpoint at provider-specified interval.
Handle device_code expiry: CLI exits non-zero with informative message; no token file written (ECT-006).
Handle unreachable device authorization endpoint: CLI displays error, exits non-zero, does not overwrite existing token file (ECT-005).
Write tests for TC-018 through TC-023, ECT-004, ECT-005, ECT-006, ST-002, ST-003.
Implement CLI session token storage: after successful login/signup, persist session token to ~/.config/af-hub/token (Linux/macOS) or %APPDATA%\af-hub\token (Windows).
Create parent directory with 0700 permissions on POSIX if it does not exist.
Create token file with 0600 permissions on POSIX (owner read/write only).
On Windows, restrict token file ACL to current user only.
If 0600 permissions cannot be set (ECT-007), display error and exit non-zero; do not create token file with insecure permissions.
Implement logout command: delete token file if present and exit 0; if file does not exist, exit 0 without error (ECT-008).
On every authenticated CLI command, read token file and pass session token as Bearer in the Authorization header.
Write tests for TC-024 through TC-028, ECT-007, ECT-008, ST-007.
Implement POST /v1/keys endpoint (authenticated users).
Require expires_at in the request body; return 422 / validation_error if absent (ECT-009).
Generate API key from minimum 32 cryptographically secure random bytes; encode as base64url.
Hash the plaintext key with bcrypt or argon2; persist only the hash along with expires_at, key_id, repo_id, created_by, created_at.
Return HTTP 201 with plaintext key and key_id in response body; plaintext is never stored or returned again.
Derive repo_id from the bearer token; grant editor role to the possessor of a valid API key for that repo.
Write tests for TC-029 (happy path), TC-030 (missing expires_at), TC-031 (editor role grant), ECT-009, and ST-004 (smoke).
Write property test PT-001 verifying base64url encoding of ≥32 bytes and hash-only storage.
Implement PATCH /v1/keys/{key_id} endpoint: only the expires_at field may be modified.
Return 422 / validation_error if request body includes fields other than expires_at (TC-034).
Return 422 / validation_error if the new expires_at is in the past (TC-035, ECT-011).
Enforce caller is key owner or admin; return 403 / permission_denied otherwise (TC-036).
Return 404 / key_not_found if key_id does not exist (TC-037).
Return HTTP 200 with updated key metadata on success (TC-032, TC-033).
Implement DELETE /v1/keys/{key_id} endpoint: immediately revoke the key with no grace period; return HTTP 200.
Enforce caller is key owner or admin on DELETE; return 403 / permission_denied otherwise (TC-039).
Return 404 / key_not_found on DELETE if key_id does not exist (TC-040).
Verify that presenting a revoked key to any endpoint immediately returns 401 / token_invalid (TC-038, ECT-010).
Write tests for TC-032 through TC-040, ECT-010, ECT-011, and ST-005 (smoke).
Write property test PT-003 verifying immediate revocation with no grace period.
Implement global error response middleware: all error responses must be application/json with body { error: '<string>', code: '<canonical-code>' }.
Enforce canonical Phase 1 error code vocabulary: token_expired, token_invalid, permission_denied, user_not_found, key_not_found, repo_not_found, conflict, validation_error, internal_error.
Return 401 / token_expired for any expired bearer token or API key within the same request cycle.
Return 401 / token_invalid for malformed, unrecognised, or revoked tokens.
Return 500 / internal_error for unexpected server errors; log full details server-side; do not expose internals in the response body.
Prefix all Phase 1 API routes with /v1/; return 404 for any path not matching a defined route (ECT-013).
Derive user_id and repo_id exclusively from the bearer token; no separate query parameters required.
Expose exactly the six Phase 1 endpoints: POST /v1/users, PATCH /v1/users/{user_id}, POST /v1/keys, PATCH /v1/keys/{key_id}, DELETE /v1/keys/{key_id}, POST /v1/admin/bootstrap-token/rotate.
Write tests for TC-041 through TC-043, TC-044, TC-045, TC-048, ECT-012, ECT-013.
Write property tests PT-002 (token_expired on every endpoint) and PT-007 (canonical error shape on all error conditions).
Ensure every database table is keyed by repo_id to enforce repo-level tenant isolation.
Scope all API requests to a single repo derived from the bearer token; return 404 / repo_not_found if the token's repo does not match the resource's repo.
Store each (user_id, repo_id) access grant as a discrete record.
Verify no cross-repo data leakage: a token scoped to repo A must never return resources belonging to repo B, even for the same user.
Write tests for TC-046 (cross-repo 404), TC-047 (no leakage with dual-repo user), and ECT-014 (repo_not_found on cross-repo access).
Write property test PT-006 verifying repo-scoped isolation across all endpoints.
Design the RBAC database schema with a roles table (or equivalent) that is extensible to additional roles and permission policies without requiring a schema migration.
Implement the single Phase 1 role: editor (full read/write access scoped to a repo).
Document schema design decisions to confirm extensibility; verify via design review rather than automated test.
Ensure machine-readable error code vocabulary is append-only in code (no removal or renaming of existing codes).
Document that TLS is required for all production deployments (any environment reachable from the public internet or external users).
Confirm TLS termination is supported at the load balancer or reverse proxy layer (e.g. nginx, AWS ALB); the application is not required to terminate TLS directly.
Local development and CI environments are exempt from TLS.
Verify TLS enforcement via infrastructure configuration audit or deployment checklist; no in-process unit test is required per coverage gaps noted in the test_spec.
Write and run all functional test cases TC-001 through TC-048 as integration tests against a real af-hub server and database instance.
Write and run property tests PT-001 through PT-008 using a property-based testing framework (e.g. hypothesis, fast-check) to verify invariants across generated inputs.
Write and run all edge-case tests ECT-001 through ECT-015.
Execute smoke tests ST-001 through ST-007 against a fully assembled environment (real server + database; OAuth provider browser leg may be mocked).
Ensure all tests pass with zero failures before marking this group done.

Confirm test coverage gaps documented in the test_spec coverage section are acknowledged: REQ-012 and REQ-013 are verified by design review/infrastructure audit rather than automated tests; Windows ACL test (TC-025) requires a Windows CI runner; OAuth provider live integration tests are deferred.

Verify that all six Phase 1 API endpoints are implemented and reachable: POST /v1/users, PATCH /v1/users/{user_id}, POST /v1/keys, PATCH /v1/keys/{key_id}, DELETE /v1/keys/{key_id}, POST /v1/admin/bootstrap-token/rotate.
Verify that a new external user can self-onboard via OAuth Device Authorization Grant and receive a working API key without admin intervention (EP-002 + EP-004).
Verify that all endpoints return HTTP 401 with token_expired or token_invalid for expired or invalid tokens within a single request cycle (PT-002, PT-003).
Verify that all error responses conform to the canonical error schema and vocabulary (PT-007, ECT-012).
Verify that API keys are stored as bcrypt/argon2 hashes only and plaintext is shown once at creation (PT-001).
Verify that repo-level tenant isolation is enforced across all endpoints with no cross-repo data leakage (PT-006).
Verify that the CLI token file is stored with 0600/0700 permissions on POSIX and restricted ACL on Windows (PT-008).
Confirm that REQ-012 (RBAC extensibility) and REQ-013 (TLS) have been reviewed and signed off via design review and infrastructure audit.
All automated tests (TC-*, PT-*, ECT-*, ST-*) pass with zero failures.
Phase 1 is considered complete only when all checks above are satisfied.
